```cpp
style.WindowPadding: 16.00,16.00

style.FrameBorderSize: 0.00

style.ItemInnerSpacing: 8.00,8.00
```



### Details:

**My Issue/Question:**

In my app, I have a hotkey to toggle hiding the UI. When it becomes visible again, the window focus isn't necessarily the same as it was previously.

I can use SetWindowFocus to correct this, but that requires knowing which window was focused when the UI became hidden. I assumed there would be a corresponding GetWindowFocus that returns the focused ID, but I don't see such a function. So I'd have to manually check IsWindowFocused after drawing every window instead, which is much less convenient as this change has to be made to a lot of different places.

### Screenshots/Video:

N/A

title: Saving .ini settings with Last Used Date information
author: ocornut
body: I'm adding a new feature and this is a general informational thread for visibility feedback. The general idea is to track and store low-footprint quantized dates in certain .ini entries: ``` [Window][Dear ImGui Demo] Pos=147,225 Size=621,987 Collapsed=0 LastUsed=20260703 <--- this ``` - When looking at .ini data it makes contexts usually easier to grasp. - If allows creating tools to manual/automatically discard old entries. Many apps can create non-finite amounts of windows/contents that never gets discarded. While the system generally holds itself well, it can adds up a bit of runtime overhead. It can also create cognitive overhead to have lots of unused data in memory/files. - Being able to have eviction policies makes it possible to consider saving more contents in .ini files (e.g. #437 but also e.g. tree nodes open state). The expectation is that people would use policies such "discard data for stuff that have been unused for 6 months". Current code enable saving/loading that data but has no eviction policy are enabled by default, and I don't expect to enable anything by default in the foreseeable future. There are corners cases related to storing persistent data (e.g. docking layouts) where I would expect feedback to happen. There is a (yet internal) `CleanupIniSettings(ImGuiSettingsCleanupArgs* args)` function that provide tools to e.g. mark data as current if needed. So if you have eviction policies you may want to load your .ini templates and apply current date to them. ### Changelog Settings: - Windows/Tables settings entries can now record the last used date in YYYYMMDD format, allowing cleanup tools to run to e.g. delete entries that haven't been used in X months. - Added `bool io.ConfigIniSettingsSaveLastUsedDate` to disable saving that info. - Added `int io.ConfigIniSettingsAutoDiscardMonths` to enable a mode where unused settings are automatically discard after xx months. - Added a trimming tool under Metrics->Settings, along with a yet-unexposed function. - The current system date is fed through `ImGuiPlatformIO::Platform_SessionDate`, which is automatically set by a call to `time()` done during context creation. - Added `IMGUI_DISABLE_TIME_FUNCTIONS` to disable setting `platform_io.Platform_SessionDate`. A custom backend may still set it manually. ### Sample .ini data: ``` [Window][Dear ImGui Demo] Pos=147,225 Size=621,987 Collapsed=0 LastUsed=20260703 [Table][0x86C9FC97,3] Column 0 Weight=1.0000 ID=0xCBD049FD Column 1 Weight=1.0000 ID=0xB39C48B7 Column 2 Weight=1.0000 ID=0x07ECE4EB LastUsed=20260702 [Table][0x95990F63,3] RefScale=23 Column 0 Width=108 ID=0xCBD049FD Column 1 Width=108 ID=0xB39C48B7 Column 2 Width=108 ID=0x07ECE4EB LastUsed=20260702 ``` ### Details The general design of the feature is: - Date footprints are 2 bytes. They are designed to be small, easy to manipulate, and easy view in debuggers. - We intentionally only store days and not time. It is smaller, but also less noisy (e.g. you don't get constant .ini changes). - We intentionally only store a unique "session" date. It is less noisy, arguably more synthetically useful, and reduces backend overhead (date is only polled once at init time). - This is optional and may be disabled, although currently I am defaulting it to enabled. In imgui_internal.h: ```cpp // [Internal] Store a date in a way that is efficient to read/write in text form. If we stored e.g. number of days since Epoch we'd need costlier back and forth. // This is specifically designed to be able to prune old .ini data. struct ImGuiPackedDate { ImU16 Year : 7; // Year since 2000 // We can change to another offset e.g. 1970 but this is easier to watch in debugger. ImU16 Month : 4; // Month (1-12) ImU16 Day : 5; // Day (1-31) ```

title: Menu Selectable Roundness
author: Pix3lP3rf3ct
body: ### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui: v1.92.9 WIP Docking ### Back-ends: imgui_impl_XXX.cpp + imgui_impl_XXX.cpp ### Compiler, OS: macOS + clang ### Full config/build information: // (Copy from the next line. Keep the ``` markers for formatting.) ```cpp Dear ImGui 1.92.9 WIP (19281) -------------------------------- sizeof(size_t): 8, sizeof(ImDrawIdx): 2, sizeof(ImDrawVert): 20 define: __cplusplus=202002 define: IMGUI_DISABLE_DEFAULT_FORMAT_FUNCTIONS define: IMGUI_DISABLE_DEFAULT_FILE_FUNCTIONS define: IMGUI_DISABLE_FILE_FUNCTIONS define: IMGUI_DISABLE_DEFAULT_ALLOCATORS define: __APPLE__ define: __GNUC__=4 define: __clang_version__=17.0.0 (clang-1700.6.3.2) define: IMGUI_HAS_VIEWPORT define: IMGUI_HAS_DOCK IM_ASSERT: runs expression: OK. expand size: OK -------------------------------- io.BackendPlatformName: NULL io.BackendRendererName: imgui_impl_TGCEngine io.ConfigFlags: 0x00000081 NavEnableKeyboard DockingEnable io.ConfigViewportsNoDecoration io.ConfigViewportsNoDefaultParent io.ConfigMacOSXBehaviors io.ConfigNavCaptureKeyboard io.ConfigInputTextCursorBlink io.ConfigWindowsResizeFromEdges io.ConfigWindowsMoveFromTitleBarOnly io.ConfigMemoryCompactTimer = 60.0 io.BackendFlags: 0x00000C18 PlatformHasViewports RendererHasVtxOffset RendererHasTextures RendererHasViewports -------------------------------- io.Fonts: 1 fonts, Flags: 0x00000000, TexSize: 512,512 io.Fonts->FontLoaderName: stb_truetype io.DisplaySize: 2822.00,1587.00 io.DisplayFramebufferScale: 2.00,2.00 -------------------------------- style.WindowPadding: 8.00,8.00 style.WindowBorderSize: 1.00 style.FramePadding: 6.00,6.00 style.FrameRounding: 4.00 style.FrameBorderSize: 1.00 style.ItemSpacing: 6.00,6.00 style.ItemInnerSpacing: 4.00,4.00 ``` ### Details: I would like the ability to specify rounding for **menu** items. Menu Items are backed by `ImGui::Selectable` so maybe the real ask is that `ImGui::Selectable` should support rounding. Most of our UI has some rounding but menu items are the only ones that have completely square edges. ### Screenshots/Video: Current: Desired: ### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code: I hard coded the `2.0f` here but it would be nice to be able to set it. ``` if (highlighted || selected) { // Between 1.91.0 and 1.91.4 we made selected Selectable use an arbitrary lerp between _Header and _HeaderHovered. Removed that now. (#8106) ImU32 col = GetColorU32((held && highlighted) ? ImGuiCol_HeaderActive : highlighted ? ImGuiCol_HeaderHovered : ImGuiCol_Header); RenderFrame(bb.Min, bb.Max, col, false, 2.0f); } ```

title: How should a Unity-style Scene View be implemented with ImGui and GLFW? (alt: Rendering to texture into a window)
author: wiltchamberian
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

docking

### Back-ends:

imgui_impl_opengl3.cpp

### Compiler, OS:

Windows10 , visual studio 2022

### Full config/build information:

I'm using ImGui with GLFW.

GLFW creates the main application window (with its own HWND on Windows), and ImGui draws multiple editor windows inside that main window.

I'm building a game engine with a Unity-like editor layout: Scene, Inspector, Assets, and Hierarchy panels. Currently, I render my scene directly onto the GLFW main window, while the Inspector, Assets, and Hierarchy are implemented as ImGui windows.

The problem is that these ImGui windows feel like floating overlays on top of the main window. In Unity, however, the Scene view is just another dockable editor window, visually consistent with the Inspector and other panels.

Because of this, it seems more reasonable to create a dedicated ImGui "Scene" window and render the scene into that window instead of rendering directly to the GLFW main window.

However, this raises another issue: input handling becomes much more complicated. Right now, mouse and keyboard events are captured directly from the GLFW main window, which makes event processing straightforward. If the scene is rendered inside an ImGui window, I'm not sure what the recommended approach is for handling mouse and keyboard input for the scene view. 

Should I:

* Continue rendering the scene directly to the GLFW main window and keep the current input model?
* Render the scene into an ImGui window (using a framebuffer texture, for example) and route input through ImGui?
* Or is there another common approach that I'm missing?

I'd like the Scene view to behave like a normal ImGui editor window while still keeping input handling simple and robust.



### Details:

**My Issue/Question:**

XXX _(please provide as much context as possible)_


### Screenshots/Video:

_No response_

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

```cpp
// Here's some code anyone can copy and paste to reproduce your issue
ImGui::Begin("Example Bug");
MoreCodeToExplainMyIssue();
ImGui::End();
```

```cpp
style.ItemSpacing: 8.00,4.00
style.ItemInnerSpacing: 4.00,4.00
```



### Details:

In commit [2e0f949039](https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/commit/2e0f9490398dd2d78ff4607cde500d581a725516#diff-7f54ef6af6357ef7c259abadb6647ce7779e355c8517f8cbb837dbe849ffe1b4R3234) a new function was added to imgui_internal.h, namely TableLoadSettingsForColumns(ImGuiTable*).
This function has no implementation, thus any attempt to call it will cause a linking error.
This is part of internal, thus less likely to be known/used, but for wrappers and such it is more likely to generate issues, like the one I have encountered with [dear_bindings](https://github.com/dearimgui/dear_bindings/issues/121)

### Screenshots/Video:

_No response_

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

```cpp
// Here's some code anyone can copy and paste to reproduce your issue
ImGui::Begin("Example Bug");
MoreCodeToExplainMyIssue();
ImGui::End();
```

title: Feature request: optional dirty/idle rendering hints for partial refresh / partial present
author: jackie-weiwei
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92

### Back-ends:

imgui_impl_win32.cpp + imgui_impl_dx11.cpp

### Compiler, OS:

windows 11

### Full config/build information:

_No response_

### Details:

Hi,

I would like to suggest an optional mechanism to help applications implement idle-aware rendering and partial refresh / partial present.

I understand that Dear ImGui is an immediate-mode UI library, and I am not suggesting that it should become a retained-mode UI framework. The idea is only to expose conservative rendering hints, so applications and backends can decide whether they need to render or present a new frame.

Some applications use Dear ImGui in environments where continuous full-frame rendering can be expensive, for example:

industrial HMI / DMI tools
embedded panels
remote desktop scenarios
software rendering / WARP devices
low-power systems
applications with mostly static UI

In these cases, rendering and presenting the entire UI at 60 FPS may be wasteful when there is no input, no animation, no window movement, and no application data change.

A possible first step could be a simple frame-level hint, for example:

bool ImGui::IsFrameRenderNeeded();

or a field in ImGuiIO / ImGuiPlatformIO, such as:

io.WantRender;

The value could be conservative:

if ImGui is unsure, return true
false positives are acceptable
false negatives should be avoided
existing applications and backends can ignore it completely

This would allow applications to do something like:

if (!ImGui::IsFrameRenderNeeded())
{
    WaitMessage();
    return;
}

ImGui::NewFrame();
BuildUI();
ImGui::Render();
RenderDrawData(ImGui::GetDrawData());
Present();

A possible second step, if it fits Dear ImGui's design, could be optional dirty rectangle hints per viewport:

int ImGui::GetDirtyRectCount();
const ImGuiDirtyRect* ImGui::GetDirtyRects();

Backends or applications could then use this information for APIs such as DXGI Present1 dirty rects, cached render textures, remote rendering, or other partial-present mechanisms.

The goal is not to make Dear ImGui own the rendering strategy. The goal is only to provide official conservative hints, so applications that care about idle rendering or partial refresh can implement it more reliably outside the core.

Thanks for considering it.
My current use case is Dear ImGui running on a DX11 WARP device, where reducing unnecessary full-frame rendering would significantly reduce CPU usage.

title: Regression in 1.92.8: docked windows with WindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize get the proportional default ItemWidthDefault
author: reybits
body: ### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui: Version 1.92.8, Branch: docking ### Back-ends: n/a - renderer-agnostic, purely a layout question ### Compiler, OS: not related to the OS or compiler ### Full config/build information: Last known good: 1.92.7-docking ## Back-ends Custom renderer (sprite batch wrapping ImDrawData). Repro is renderer-agnostic - purely a layout question. ## Compiler, OS macOS 14, Apple Clang 17, C++17. Behavior is platform-independent. ## Full config/build information ```cpp IMGUI_DISABLE_OBSOLETE_FUNCTIONS, IMGUI_DEFINE_MATH_OPERATORS, IMGUI_DISABLE_DEMO_WINDOWS. ImGuiBackendFlags_RendererHasTextures set, ImGuiConfigFlags_DockingEnable set. ``` ### Details: ## Details In 1.92.8, windows opened with `ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize` no longer get the fixed `ItemWidthDefault = FontSize * 16.0f` when they are docked into a dockspace. Instead they fall through to the resizable-width path (`ItemWidthDefault = Size.x * 0.65`), so all default-width widgets shrink to 65% of the dock tab's width with empty space to the right. This was working as expected in 1.92.7. ## Root cause Commit 95bd1577d ("Windows: Child windows with only `ImGuiChildFlags_AutoResizeY` flag keep using the proportional default ItemWidth", #9355) restructured the is_resizable_width decision into two branches: ```cpp // imgui.cpp Begin() — current 1.92.8 bool is_resizable_width; if (flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_ChildWindow) is_resizable_width = (window->Size.x > 0.0f) && !(window->ChildFlags & (ImGuiChildFlags_AutoResizeX | ImGuiChildFlags_AlwaysAutoResize)); else is_resizable_width = (window->Size.x > 0.0f) && !(flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize); ``` The ChildWindow branch consults `window->ChildFlags` exclusively. It does not check `flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize`. When a window is docked, the docking code adds `ImGuiWindowFlags_ChildWindow` to its Flags, while the user-supplied `ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize` stays on Flags (not promoted to ChildFlags). The window then enters the ChildWindow branch, the only AlwaysAutoResize-bearing flag check is skipped, and the window is classified as resizable for item-width purposes - opposite of what the user requested. ## Repro ```cpp ImGui::DockSpaceOverViewport(0, nullptr, ImGuiDockNodeFlags_PassthruCentralNode); ImGui::Begin("Tools", nullptr, ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize | ImGuiWindowFlags_NoCollapse); float v = 0.5f; ImGui::SliderFloat("##slider", &v, 0.0f, 1.0f); // expect FontSize*16 fixed width ImGui::InputText("##input", buf, sizeof(buf)); // expect FontSize*16 fixed width ImGui::End(); ``` Dock "Tools" into the dockspace, then resize the tab pane. Each widget grows/shrinks to tab_size.x * 0.65. ## Expected AlwaysAutoResize should still produce a fixed `ItemWidthDefault = FontSize * 16` regardless of whether the window is currently free-floating, dragged onto a tab, or already docked. ## Suggested minimal fix Honor `WindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize` in the ChildWindow branch too: ```cpp - if (flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_ChildWindow) - is_resizable_width = (window->Size.x > 0.0f) && !(window->ChildFlags & (ImGuiChildFlags_AutoResizeX | ImGuiChildFlags_AlwaysAutoResize)); + if (flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_ChildWindow) + is_resizable_width = (window->Size.x > 0.0f) + && !(flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize) + && !(window->ChildFlags & (ImGuiChildFlags_AutoResizeX | ImGuiChildFlags_AlwaysAutoResize)); else is_resizable_width = (window->Size.x > 0.0f) && !(flags & ImGuiWindowFlags_AlwaysAutoResize); ``` This restores 1.92.7 semantics for docked AlwaysAutoResize windows without affecting the #9355 fix (ImGuiChildFlags_AutoResizeY-only child windows continue to use the proportional default). ## Adjacent observation (optional) While investigating, we noticed the proportional `ItemWidthDefault = Size.x * 0.65f` leaves 35% empty space on the right when widgets are used with "`##id`"-style labels (empty visible text). The widget shrinks to 65% but no label is rendered to fill the remainder. This isn't the regression itself, but combined with AlwaysAutoResize-docked windows it doubles the surprise. Would it be in scope to make widgets expand to fill the remaining row width when the rendered label width is 0? Happy to file as a separate issue if useful. ### Screenshots/Video: ### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code: _No response_

title: GLFW multi-viewport position mismatch when window manager clamps secondary viewport
author: KBentley57
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8, Branch: docking

### Back-ends:

`imgui_impl_glfw.cpp` + `imgui_impl_opengl3.cpp`

### Compiler, OS:

Linux/X11, observed with GLFW + OpenGL3. This is easiest to reproduce with a window manager that clamps programmatic window positions near monitor edges, such as GNOME Shell/Mutter.

I have confirmed the issue across Dear ImGui versions from at least 1.90 through current docking HEAD, using GCC 13 and GCC 14. It has been reproduced on Debian 13, Ubuntu 24.04, Ubuntu under WSL, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9, Rocky Linux 8, and Rocky Linux 9.

### Full config/build information:

The repro enables:

```cpp
io.ConfigFlags |= ImGuiConfigFlags_NavEnableKeyboard;
io.ConfigFlags |= ImGuiConfigFlags_DockingEnable;
io.ConfigFlags |= ImGuiConfigFlags_ViewportsEnable;
```

The detached test window uses `ImGuiViewportFlags_NoAutoMerge` through `ImGuiWindowClass` so only that window becomes a secondary platform viewport.

### Details:

When a secondary GLFW platform viewport is moved or programmatically requested near a monitor edge, the window manager may clamp the actual platform window position. Dear ImGui may continue to store the requested position in `viewport->Pos`, while `glfwGetWindowPos()` reports the actual clamped position.

Once those positions diverge, hit-testing/rendering in the secondary viewport can appear offset from the mouse cursor. Moving the viewport back into the interior of the monitor usually clears the mismatch.

The relevant backend behavior appears to be in `ImGui_ImplGlfw_WindowPosCallback()`. The backend intentionally ignores window-position callbacks for one frame after `glfwSetWindowPos()` so it does not process ordinary echo events. That makes sense when GLFW reports the same position Dear ImGui requested. But on X11, the callback may report a different position because the window manager clamped the platform window. Ignoring that callback leaves Dear ImGui synchronized to the requested position instead of the real platform position.

I opened a small PR that keeps the existing ignore behavior for exact echo events, but treats a different reported position as a real platform move:

https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/pull/9441

Related reports/discussion:

- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/issues/9356
- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/issues/9398
- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/issues/8330
- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/issues/8251

### Screenshots/Video:

The repro displays both Dear ImGui's cached viewport position and the actual GLFW platform window position, which makes the mismatch visible numerically.

Normal expected layout: the main controls viewport and detached secondary viewport are both visible, and the secondary viewport reports `Delta: (0, 0)` / `OK`.

![Normal layout with matching cached and platform positions](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KBentley57/imgui-viewport-edge-repro/main/screenshots/01-normal-ok.png)

Bottom/left edge clamp on the affected backend: the detached viewport is clamped by the window manager, while Dear ImGui still has the requested position cached. The table shows `MISMATCH`, cached `(-61, 1224)`, platform `(0, 1128)`, delta `(61, 96)`.

![Bottom edge mismatch showing cached and platform position delta](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KBentley57/imgui-viewport-edge-repro/main/screenshots/02-bottom-edge-mismatch.png)

Top edge clamp on the affected backend: the same mismatch appears with a smaller top-edge delta, cached `(958, -9)`, platform `(959, 0)`.

![Top edge mismatch showing cached and platform position delta](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KBentley57/imgui-viewport-edge-repro/main/screenshots/03-top-edge-mismatch.png)

Expected corrected behavior: after synchronizing to the actual platform position, the cached and GLFW platform positions match again and the status returns to `OK`.

![Corrected behavior with matching cached and platform positions](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KBentley57/imgui-viewport-edge-repro/main/screenshots/04-sync-workaround-ok.png)

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

Minimal repro repository:

https://github.com/KBentley57/imgui-viewport-edge-repro

Build it against an unmodified `v1.92.8-docking` checkout:

```sh
cmake -S . \
      -B /tmp/imgui_viewport_edge_repro_build \
      -DIMGUI_SOURCE_DIR=/path/to/imgui \
      -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=/path/to/glfw/prefix
cmake --build /tmp/imgui_viewport_edge_repro_build -j"$(nproc)"
/tmp/imgui_viewport_edge_repro_build/imgui_viewport_edge_repro
```

Steps:

1. Leave `Apply platform position sync workaround` unchecked.
2. Click `Spawn / reset detached viewport`.
3. Click `Request bottom-edge clamp` or drag the detached OS window into a monitor edge.
4. Compare `Cached viewport pos` with `GLFW platform pos`.
5. Build the same repro against the PR branch and repeat.

With the affected backend, the cached and platform positions can diverge near the edge. With the PR branch, the backend should accept the clamped/different callback and converge back to the actual platform position.

title: Keyboard navigation rectangle not drawn correctly
author: TadGit
body: ### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui: v1.92.9 docking ### Back-ends: imgui-SFML ### Compiler, OS: Windows 10 - VS2026 ### Full config/build information: // (Copy from the next line. Keep the ``` markers for formatting.) ```cpp Dear ImGui 1.92.9 WIP (19281) -------------------------------- sizeof(size_t): 8, sizeof(ImDrawIdx): 2, sizeof(ImDrawVert): 20 define: __cplusplus=199711 define: _WIN32 define: _WIN64 define: _MSC_VER=1951 define: _MSVC_LANG=202002 define: IMGUI_HAS_VIEWPORT define: IMGUI_HAS_DOCK IM_ASSERT: runs expression: OK. expand size: OK -------------------------------- io.BackendPlatformName: imgui_impl_sfml io.BackendRendererName: NULL io.ConfigFlags: 0x00000081 NavEnableKeyboard DockingEnable io.ConfigViewportsNoDecoration io.ConfigViewportsNoDefaultParent io.ConfigNavCaptureKeyboard io.ConfigInputTextCursorBlink io.ConfigWindowsResizeFromEdges io.ConfigMemoryCompactTimer = 60.0 io.BackendFlags: 0x00000007 HasGamepad HasMouseCursors HasSetMousePos -------------------------------- io.Fonts: 1 fonts, Flags: 0x00000000, TexSize: 512,128 io.Fonts->FontLoaderName: stb_truetype io.DisplaySize: 1920.00,1017.00 io.DisplayFramebufferScale: 1.00,1.00 -------------------------------- style.WindowPadding: 8.00,8.00 style.WindowBorderSize: 1.00 style.FramePadding: 4.00,3.00 style.FrameRounding: 0.00 style.FrameBorderSize: 0.00 style.ItemSpacing: 8.00,4.00 style.ItemInnerSpacing: 4.00,4.00 ``` ### Details: I've noticed that my ImGui keyboard navigation rectangle is not always drawn correctly. The error is small, barely visible, just a few pixels out of place. I saw the ImGui article "Pixel Perfect Rendering" and I thought it might have something to do with this. But, even with anti-aliasing on, the result is the same. I also thought it might be the backend (SFML). But, I don't see this effect in other lines or rectangles. I looked through the sorce code but, I couldn't find where the rectangle is drawn. Any ideas on why this is happening or the location of the rectangle's drawing procedure? Here's a magnified ImGui::Button to illustrate - ### Screenshots/Video: _No response_ ### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code: _No response_

title: ImFontConfig::FontLoaderData should not be required to be NULL
author: sturnclaw
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8, Branch: docking

### Back-ends:

imgui_impl_sdl2.cpp + custom renderer backend

### Compiler, OS:

Linux GCC

### Full config/build information:

_No response_

### Details:

**My Issue/Question:**

`ImFontAtlas::AddFont()` asserts that the `FontLoaderData` member of `ImFontConfig` is == NULL when adding the font. This is a strongly undesired behavior, as it prevents the implementation calling `AddFont` from passing data to the font loader to be used at load time.

We currently use a custom `FontLoaderData` parameter created when calling `AddFont` to pass information about the font file (in our case, an SVG file containing gridded icons) to the custom loader responsible for rasterizing and uploading the texture data to the font atlas.

Consequently, this assert triggers during startup and crashes the application. As a workaround, we simply disable this assert at compile time.

### Screenshots/Video:

_No response_

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

```cpp
// Here's some code anyone can copy and paste to reproduce your issue
ImFontConfig cfg;
cfg.FontLoader = makeMyLoader();
cfg.FontLoaderData = (void *) new MyLoaderData();
io.Fonts->AddFont(&cfg); // <-- CRASH!
```

title: Dawn examples require C++20 compiler
author: oliverdixon
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8, Branch: master

### Back-ends:

imgui_impl_wgpu.cpp

### Compiler, OS:

Linux + Clang 21.1.8 (6ubuntu1)

### Full config/build information:

_No response_

### Details:

**My Issue/Question:**

Since 18th April 2025, Google's WebGPU implementation _Dawn_ requires a C++20 compiler.  The change can be seen here: https://github.com/google/dawn/commit/32c8fdb708ef1f074fad902247e1280f9aa2eb8b

Three _Dear ImGui_ examples build Dawn in the project directory:

- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/tree/master/examples/example_glfw_wgpu
- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/tree/master/examples/example_sdl2_wgpu
- https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/tree/master/examples/example_sdl3_wgpu

In each case, `dawn/webgpu_cpp.h` (generated at build-time) fails to build due to use of C++20 features, where the `CMakeLists.txt` sets `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD` to `17`.

Sample of build errors:

```
/home/owd/Documents/imgui/examples/example_glfw_wgpu/dawn/gen/include/dawn/webgpu_cpp.h:4327:47: warning: explicit template parameter list for lambdas is a C++20 extension [-Wc++20-extensions]
 4327 |     using BaseArgsTuple = typename decltype([]<std::size_t... Is>(std::index_sequence<Is...>) {
      |                                               ^
/home/owd/Documents/imgui/examples/example_glfw_wgpu/dawn/gen/include/dawn/webgpu_cpp.h:4328:21: error: no template named 'type_identity' in namespace 'std'; did you mean '__type_identity'?
 4328 |         return std::type_identity<std::tuple<std::tuple_element_t<Is, std::tuple<CppArgs...>>...>>{};
      |                ~~~~~^~~~~~~~~~~~~
      |                     __type_identity
/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/15/../../../../include/c++/15/type_traits:167:12: note: '__type_identity' declared here
  167 |     struct __type_identity
      |            ^
In file included from /home/owd/Documents/imgui/examples/example_glfw_wgpu/main.cpp:27:
In file included from /home/owd/Documents/webcfd/dawn/include/webgpu/webgpu_cpp.h:31:
/home/owd/Documents/imgui/examples/example_glfw_wgpu/dawn/gen/include/dawn/webgpu_cpp.h:4340:47: warning: explicit template parameter list for lambdas is a C++20 extension [-Wc++20-extensions]
 4340 |     using BaseArgsTuple = typename decltype([]<std::size_t... Is>(std::index_sequence<Is...>) {
      |                                               ^
/home/owd/Documents/imgui/examples/example_glfw_wgpu/dawn/gen/include/dawn/webgpu_cpp.h:4341:21: error: no template named 'type_identity' in namespace 'std'; did you mean '__type_identity'?
 4341 |         return std::type_identity<std::tuple<std::tuple_element_t<Is, std::tuple<CppArgs...>>...>>{};
      |                ~~~~~^~~~~~~~~~~~~
      |                     __type_identity
/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/15/../../../../include/c++/15/type_traits:167:12: note: '__type_identity' declared here
  167 |     struct __type_identity
      |            ^
```

Therefore, in each of the WebGPU examples which rely on Dawn, `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD` should be set to at least `20`.

### Screenshots/Video:

_No response_

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

_No response_

title: example_sdl3_wgpu : Building for desktop with WGVK fails with various errors
author: shanemcandrewai
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8, Branch: master

### Back-ends:

imgui_impl_sdl3.cpp + imgui_impl_wgpu.cpp

### Compiler, OS:

Windows 11, clang version 22.1.1, Windows 11 SDK (10.0.26100.775)

### Full config/build information:




### Details:

Following the [instructions # Building for desktop with WGVK (MUCH EASIER)](https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/blob/master/examples/example_sdl3_wgpu/CMakeLists.txt) -

1. git clone https://github.com/manuel5975p/WGVK dawn
2. cmake -B build -DIMGUI_WGVK_DIR=wgvk
3. cmake --build build

Is there a typo in point 1? I tried git cloning WGVK  to both `dawn` and `wgvk` but failed to generate the build files in both cases with step 2, error below -
```
CMake Error at C:/Program Files/CMake/share/cmake-4.3/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:290 (message):
  Could NOT find Vulkan (missing: Vulkan_LIBRARY Vulkan_INCLUDE_DIR) (found
  version "")
Call Stack (most recent call first):
  C:/Program Files/CMake/share/cmake-4.3/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:654 (_FPHSA_FAILURE_MESSAGE)
  C:/Program Files/CMake/share/cmake-4.3/Modules/FindVulkan.cmake:711 (find_package_handle_standard_args)
  CMakeLists.txt:150 (find_package)
```
To overcome this error, I first built the `wgvk` repo, then executed -

    cmake -B build -DIMGUI_WGVK_DIR=wgvk -DVulkan_LIBRARY=wgvk/build/Release -DVulkan_LIBRARY=wgvk/include -G "Ninja Multi-Config"

this ran without error but I when I tried to build with `cmake --build build`, it failed with -

```
[10/10] Linking CXX executable Debug\example_sdl3_wgpu.exe
FAILED: [code=1] Debug/example_sdl3_wgpu.exe 
C:\WINDOWS\system32\cmd.exe /C "cd . && C:\PROGRA~1\LLVM\bin\CLANG_~1.EXE -nostartfiles -nostdlib -O0 -g -Xclang -gcodeview -D_DEBUG -D_DLL -D_MT -Xclang --dependent-lib=msvcrtd -Xlinker /subsystem:console -fuse-ld=lld-link CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/main.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/backends/imgui_impl_sdl3.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/backends/imgui_impl_wgpu.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/imgui.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/imgui_draw.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/imgui_demo.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/imgui_tables.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/C_/Users/shane/dev/imgui/imgui_widgets.cpp.obj CMakeFiles/example_sdl3_wgpu.dir/Debug/wgvk/src/wgvk.c.obj -o Debug\example_sdl3_wgpu.exe -Xlinker /MANIFEST:EMBED -Xlinker /implib:Debug\example_sdl3_wgpu.lib -Xlinker /pdb:Debug\example_sdl3_wgpu.pdb -Xlinker /version:0.0   -lglfw.lib  C:/Users/shane/dev/SDL3/imgui_sdl3_wgpu/wgvk/build/Release  C:/Users/shane/dev/SDL3/SDL3-3.4.4/lib/x64/SDL3.lib  -lkernel32 -luser32 -lgdi32 -lwinspool -lshell32 -lole32 -loleaut32 -luuid -lcomdlg32 -ladvapi32 -loldnames  && cd ."
lld-link: error: could not open 'glfw.lib': no such file or directory
clang++: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
ninja: build stopped: subcommand failed.
```

Do I really need ```glfw.lib```? Where did I go wrong?

Extra info: I was able to successfully build [example_sdl3_wgpu](https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/tree/master/examples/example_sdl3_wgpu) with Dawn and Emscripten following the instructions with only a few tweaks to the ```CMakeLists.txt```

### Screenshots/Video:

_No response_

### Minimal, Complete and Verifiable Example code:

```cpp
// Here's some code anyone can copy and paste to reproduce your issue
ImGui::Begin("Example Bug");
MoreCodeToExplainMyIssue();
ImGui::End();
```

title: Question: ownership semantics for user-instanced shared `ImFontAtlas` across multiple contexts
author: ambiennt
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8, Branch: master

### Back-ends:

Custom

### Compiler, OS:

Windows 10 amd64 + MSVC/Clang-cl

### Full config/build information:

```
IMGUI_DEFINE_MATH_OPERATORS
IMGUI_DISABLE_OBSOLETE_FUNCTIONS
IMGUI_DISABLE_WIN32_FUNCTIONS
IMGUI_DISABLE_DEFAULT_SHELL_FUNCTIONS
IMGUI_DISABLE_FILE_FUNCTIONS
```

### Details:

**My Issue/Question:**

I am trying to use two ImGui contexts that share a single manually-created font atlas. My goal is to render one context before part of my host application's UI and another context after it, while sharing the same fonts/atlas between both contexts. All my fonts are uncompressed and embedded in my application as static storage.

The setup is roughly:

```cpp
shared_font_atlas = IM_NEW(ImFontAtlas)();
ImFontConfig cfg{};
cfg.FontDataOwnedByAtlas = false;

shared_font_atlas->AddFontFromMemoryTTF(..., &cfg);

bottom_ctx = ImGui::CreateContext(shared_font_atlas);
top_ctx = ImGui::CreateContext(shared_font_atlas);
```

Since the atlas is manually managed, I am also calling:
```cpp
ImFontAtlasUpdateNewFrame(
    shared_font_atlas,
    frame_count, // manually incremented per host application frame
    renderer_has_textures // true
);
```
once per outer application frame before calling `ImGui::NewFrame()` on either context.

Both contexts are initialized with the same renderer backend, and both contexts have matching backend flags, including `ImGuiBackendFlags_RendererHasTextures`.

My question is about the intended ownership and destruction semantics:
When an `ImFontAtlas*` is passed to `ImGui::CreateContext(shared_font_atlas)`, am I correct that the context only borrows the atlas and will not destroy it?
If the atlas was allocated with `IM_NEW(ImFontAtlas)()`, is the caller expected to destroy it with `IM_DELETE(shared_font_atlas)` after destroying all contexts that use it?

Is the expected destruction order:

```cpp
ImGui::DestroyContext(top_ctx);
ImGui::DestroyContext(bottom_ctx);
IM_DELETE(shared_font_atlas); // crash
```

Are there any additional cleanup steps required for a manually-managed shared atlas, especially when using the 1.92+ texture API / `ImGuiBackendFlags_RendererHasTextures`?
Is it safe/recommended to manage the atlas with a `std::unique_ptr<ImFontAtlas, custom_deleter>` where the deleter calls `IM_DELETE`, or is raw pointer ownership preferred for this case?

I am asking because using a raw pointer allocated with `IM_NEW(ImFontAtlas)()` appears to work *as long as I do not explicitly destroy the atlas*, but explicitly deleting it after destroying both contexts crashes in my application with what appears to be a double-free segfault [here](https://github.com/ocornut/imgui/blob/5aa0393a15f42f3d9274f6f9ea540b0b0e448ac5/imgui.cpp#L1473). I want to confirm whether this indicates a bug in my destruction order / frame lifetime, or whether I am misunderstanding ownership expectations for shared manually-managed font atlases.

title: Compilation error on MSYS2/MinGW-64 with 1.92.8
author: encelo
body:
### Version/Branch of Dear ImGui:

Version 1.92.8

### Back-ends:

Custom back-end (nCine OpenGL)

### Compiler, OS:

MSYS2 GCC 16.1.0

### Full config/build information:

MSYS2 updated with pacman, running on Windows 11.
Compiling code with GCC 16.1.0.

### Details:

I was recently updating the ImGui backend of the [nCine](https://ncine.github.io/) from 1.92.7 to 1.92.8.

In my `imconfig.h` I have, among others, the following definitions:

```cpp
#define IMGUI_API DLL_PUBLIC
#define IMGUI_DISABLE_OBSOLETE_FUNCTIONS
```

When I try to compile some fo the tests for my framework (executables that link to the nCine DLL) on MSYS2, I get those errors:

```bash
[3/46] Building CXX object tests/CMakeFiles/apptest_particles.dir/apptest_particles.cpp.obj
FAILED: [code=1] tests/CMakeFiles/apptest_particles.dir/apptest_particles.cpp.obj
C:\msys64\mingw64\bin\c++.exe -DNCINE_DEBUG -DNCINE_TESTS_DATA_DIR=\"C:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/nCine-data/\" -IC:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/DELETEME/imgui-1.92.8/include_only -IC:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/nCine-imgui/include -IC:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/DELETEME/generated/include -g -MD -MT tests/CMakeFiles/apptest_particles.dir/apptest_particles.cpp.obj -MF tests\CMakeFiles\apptest_particles.dir\apptest_particles.cpp.obj.d -o tests/CMakeFiles/apptest_particles.dir/apptest_particles.cpp.obj -c C:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/nCine-imgui/tests/apptest_particles.cpp
In file included from C:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/nCine-imgui/tests/apptest_particles.cpp:15:
C:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/DELETEME/imgui-1.92.8/include_only/ncine/imgui.h:3424:21: error: function 'void ImDrawList::AddRect(const ImVec2&, const ImVec2&, ImU32, float, ImDrawFlags, float)' definition is marked dllimport
 3424 |     IMGUI_API void  AddRect(const ImVec2& p_min, const ImVec2& p_max, ImU32 col, float rounding /*= 0.0f*/, ImDrawFlags flags /*= 0*/, float thickness /*= 1.0f*/) = delete;
      |                     ^~~~~~~
C:/msys64/home/encelo/nCine/DELETEME/imgui-1.92.8/include_only/ncine/imgui.h:3425:21: error: function 'void ImDrawList::AddPolyline(const ImVec2*, int, ImU32, ImDrawFlags, float)' definition is marked dllimport
 3425 |     IMGUI_API void  AddPolyline(const ImVec2* points, int num_points, ImU32 col, ImDrawFlags flags, float thickness) = delete;
      |                     ^~~~~~~~~~~
ninja: build stopped: subcommand failed.
```

The issue seems to be related to using `IMGUI_API` with those deleted functions:

```cpp
IMGUI_API void  AddRect(const ImVec2& p_min, const ImVec2& p_max, ImU32 col, float rounding /*= 0.0f*/, ImDrawFlags flags /*= 0*/, float thickness /*= 1.0f*/) = delete;
IMGUI_API void  AddPolyline(const ImVec2* points, int num_points, ImU32 col, ImDrawFlags flags, float thickness) = delete;
```

If I remove the `IMGUI_API` decorations from the functions then the code compiles as expected.
